speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six, interview with Vera Ray.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a photo of my grandmother and my grandfather, my maternal grandmother and grandfather. And I don't know where these photos come from. I think we must have taken them with us when we-- when we fled Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandfather died when my mother was five years old. And so it would have been taken 80 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chatsa, I assume.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --grandfather and grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ella Volkman And I don't remember his name. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is also a photograph of my maternal grandmother. And it's how I remember her in, you know, in the late '30s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my mother as a teenager, top row, center. And her brother is next to her on her right. Her brother's name is Martin.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, my mother was a teenager. She would be 84 now or 85 if she were alive. So I'm no good at arithmetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where is it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say Chatsa, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure. I think they-- it looks like-- she used to talk about dancing school. And they all seem to be paired off. So I would-- I think it could have been dancing school. I'm not sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother and me. And I must have been about three years old, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Orlova.

speaker: interviewer
text: You seem to have very warm relationship there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We loved each other. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's my father in his uniform. This is the Czech-- the uniform of the Czech army in Russia. And as you can see, he has some medals there. Both my parents were pretty brave. And I'm sure they deserved their medals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think possibly the-- because it looks so-- such a good uniform, it must have been taken in '45, once we go-- after the war, once we got to Czechoslovakia. I can't remember them having good uniforms like that. That's my mother in her uniform. She also has some award ribbons. She also had some medals. And we'll be showing those medals later on after we've seen the pictures. That is also 1945. And I would say that it would be straight after the war, because she was demobbed fairly soon afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father. I think he's being-- getting an award or a medal. And he's the person whose hand is being shaken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that, again, these pictures are taken directly after the war. And it's in-- I think it's in Chomutov. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my mother and I. And it has to be around about Buzuluk. And we got the uniforms from-- from the British forces. And some of the uniforms were cut down so that the children of the soldiers could also wear those uniforms. And sometimes they trained us to march and do all this soldierly stuff. So we felt quite good in those uniforms. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a photo of my children. Vivian, she would have been about 3 and a 1/2 there, and Tony would have been 18 months old. And they're cute kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Tony when he was 14 years old. He has red hair, and blue eyes, and freckles. He was a good-looking boy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's Viv, Vivian, when she was 16 years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is me and Pia, and Omi's taking a photograph of us together.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] Yes. I bought them pajamas that are exactly the same so that they don't have any reason to fight. And it seems to work. They seem to like each other in that photograph. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is me and Omi on our holiday. And Pia was there as well, and so was Jenny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Six.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a card that I picked up when I was in Prague after 40 years. And it really made me feel that I have not lost my Czech identity, because it-- I felt very emotional that the Dubcek-- that during Dubcek's era, they had to fight so hard. And they lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: '68. Oh, the Dubcek thing was in '68. But I was there a couple of years ago. So the-- it must-- about '94. This is another card. And it says, Praha, my love. And that's exactly what it is. I never stopped identifying with Prague. And this is one of the things that the war has robbed me from. And that is my citizenship of my country. These are some of the medals that my parents earned. As you can see, two of them are the same, so I'm assuming that they had one each. The other medals, I can't really remember which is which. But there is one badge there that my father wore very proudly, and it's the Australian RSL badge, the returned soldiers. He marched every time for Anzac Day, and he really considered himself, as he should have, that he was a returned soldier. But he was glad that-- that the RSL permitted foreign soldiers to march also. So both my parents must have been brave. They must have done something very brave to have received all these medals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
